heroOverview: Pyromania (ICD-10: F63.1) is a rare impulse-control disorder defined by recurrent, deliberate fire-setting preceded by a mounting tension or affective arousal, and followed by a sense of relief, gratification, or pleasure at the time of setting or witnessing the fire. The distinguishing feature is that the fire-setting is an end in itself — not motivated by financial gain, ideology, concealment of a crime, or psychotic command — and is accompanied by a specific fascination with fire. Epidemiological data consistently indicate that true pyromania meeting strict diagnostic criteria is uncommon among fire-setters; studies estimate that fewer than 3–4% of individuals arrested for arson meet full pyromania criteria when carefully assessed, though fire-setting behaviour broadly is far more prevalent. The condition affects a predominance of males and typically emerges in childhood or early adolescence. Evidence-based treatment combines cognitive-behavioural therapy addressing urge management with pharmacotherapy for co-occurring psychiatric conditions.

keyStats.prevalence: True pyromania meeting strict criteria present in fewer than 3–4% of arson arrestees; fire-setting broadly occurs in approximately 1% of community samples (Lejoyeux et al., Comprehensive Psychiatry 2002)
keyStats.demographics: Predominantly male; onset typically in childhood or early adolescence; over-represented in forensic and correctional populations
keyStats.avgAge: Peak onset in late childhood and adolescence; adult-onset cases exist but are less common
keyStats.globalCases: Precise global prevalence not established; classified as rare in epidemiological surveys of impulse-control disorders

definition: Pyromania (ICD-10: F63.1) is classified within the group of habit and impulse disorders and is characterised by multiple episodes of deliberate and purposeful fire-setting, accompanied by: intense interest in or attraction to fire and its situational contexts; a building tension or emotional arousal before the act; gratification, relief, or pleasure at the moment of fire-setting or when witnessing or participating in its aftermath; and the absence of obvious motivators such as financial gain, ideological expression, concealment of a crime, vengeance, or response to psychotic delusion or hallucination. The criterion that fire-setting is intrinsically rewarding and driven by the impulse itself is central to distinguishing pyromania from the much larger category of deliberate fire-setting for instrumental purposes. Although the DSM-5 and ICD-10 criteria are similar, the ICD-10 diagnosis (F63.1) requires multiple acts and the specific affective pattern of tension–pleasure–relief. The condition is categorised alongside kleptomania and intermittent explosive disorder within impulse-control disorder classifications. Pyromania is often associated with co-occurring attention deficit hyperactivity disorder, learning disabilities, substance use disorders, and other impulse-control disorders. Forensically, fire-setting regardless of motivation is a high-risk behaviour, and careful assessment to determine whether fire-setting is pyromanic, instrumentally motivated, or related to another psychiatric disorder is essential for risk management.

typesClassification.description: Pure pyromania as defined by ICD-10: recurrent fire-setting driven exclusively by tension-relief-fascination arousal with no instrumental motivation, in the absence of another major psychiatric disorder explaining the behaviour.

typesClassification.type: Pyromania with ADHD or neurodevelopmental comorbidity
typesClassification.description: The most common clinical presentation; fire-setting is driven by the core pyromanic impulse but amplified by ADHD-related impulsivity and difficulty sustaining inhibition; this subtype responds to combined management of both conditions.

typesClassification.type: Pyromania with co-occurring substance use disorder
typesClassification.description: Substance intoxication — particularly alcohol — significantly disinhibits the pyromanic impulse; many fire-setting episodes occur during or after alcohol or drug use in this subtype.

typesClassification.type: Fire-setting behaviour secondary to another disorder
typesClassification.description: Not true pyromania: fire-setting in the context of conduct disorder, antisocial personality, psychosis, intellectual disability, or substance intoxication is diagnostically distinct and requires different management, though risk management principles overlap.

primarySymptoms: Recurrent deliberate fire-setting on multiple occasions, not explainable by any instrumental motivation, emotional crisis at the time, or other psychiatric condition commanding the behaviour.
Mounting internal tension, restlessness, or affective arousal in the period before setting a fire, qualitatively described as a building drive or pressure that demands release.
Gratification, relief, pleasure, or excitement at the time of fire-setting or when witnessing, participating in, or learning about the fire's consequences.
Specific and persistent fascination with fire itself — its appearance, sensory properties, and the broader cultural and situational contexts of fire, including emergency response activities.
Preoccupation with fire and fire-related subjects between episodes: spending disproportionate time around fires, at fire stations, or consuming fire-related media.
Failure to resist the impulse to set fires despite awareness of the associated risk and negative consequences, consistent with the defining characteristic of an impulse-control disorder.
Absence of clear remorse or guilt after fire-setting in the acute phase, unlike in OCD or other conditions where distress follows the compulsive act — though secondary guilt may emerge.
No financial, ideological, or practical motivation for the fire-setting; the act is experienced as gratifying in itself.
Possible fascination with firefighting equipment, uniforms, or emergency services, and a tendency to loiter at fire scenes after the event.
Co-occurring impulse-control difficulties in other domains — spending, aggression, or substance use — reflecting a broader pattern of reduced behavioural inhibition.
earlyWarningSigns: Escalating preoccupation with fire, fire-related content, or activities involving flames that is disproportionate to age and context.
A history of small experimental fires in childhood with an affective component of excitement beyond typical childhood curiosity.
Reports of building tension that is specifically relieved by observing or setting fires, as distinct from general anxiety.
Loitering at fire scenes or seeking access to fire stations, fire-fighting equipment, or fire-related occupations out of proportion to any vocational interest.
emergencySigns: Any active fire-setting posing risk to life, property, or infrastructure — requires immediate police and fire-service response and emergency psychiatric assessment.
Disclosure of imminent plans to set a fire — a psychiatric emergency requiring urgent clinical risk assessment and, where appropriate, notification of authorities.
Significant escalation in frequency or scale of fire-setting behaviour in a known patient — risk of serious harm to others requires emergency management review.
Suicidal fire-setting or severe self-harm in the context of pyromania or a co-occurring disorder — emergency psychiatric intervention is required.

causes.description: Pyromania involves a failure of the inhibitory systems that normally prevent acting on arousal-building impulses; neuropsychological and imaging studies in impulse-control disorders suggest reduced prefrontal cortical regulation of subcortical reward and arousal circuits, a pattern consistent with the tension–relief cycle of pyromania.

Early experiences of excitement, relief, or fascination associated with fire — whether through accidental exposure, deliberate experimentation, or family modelling — establish conditioned associations between fire-related stimuli and powerful positive affect that are subsequently strengthened by repeated exposure.

causes.cause: Adverse childhood experiences and emotional dysregulation
causes.description: Fire-setting in children and adolescents is strongly associated with neglect, abuse, family instability, and inadequate emotional support; fire may function as a dysregulated expression of distress or a bid for control in an environment perceived as chaotic and unresponsive.

Elevated impulsivity as a trait — associated with lower monoaminergic tone, particularly serotonergic and dopaminergic signalling in prefrontal circuits — is a consistent finding across impulse-control disorders and is likely to contribute to the failure to inhibit the fire-setting impulse once tension builds.

Pyromania and fire-setting are over-represented in individuals with intellectual disability and learning difficulties, where reduced capacity to process emotional states verbally and to anticipate consequences increases the probability of behavioural expression of tension.

Exposure to fire-setting by family members, peers, or through media — particularly during developmentally sensitive periods — can establish fire as a salient and affectively charged behavioural option in susceptible individuals.

riskFactors.description: Pyromania and fire-setting are strongly male-predominant in both clinical and forensic populations; males account for over 85% of arson arrestees across multiple national statistics.
Onset of fire-setting in childhood, especially with an affective component, substantially predicts persistence into adolescence and adulthood; early onset is the strongest developmental risk factor.

ADHD is the most consistently identified psychiatric comorbidity in pyromania and fire-setting populations; the shared substrate of impulsivity amplifies the difficulty of resisting the fire-setting impulse when tension builds.
Physical abuse, neglect, household dysfunction, and early exposure to traumatic events are significantly over-represented in fire-setter histories compared to non-fire-setting populations.

Alcohol and stimulant intoxication dramatically reduce inhibitory control and are associated with a substantial proportion of fire-setting episodes in vulnerable individuals; reducing substance use is a critical modifiable risk factor.
Fire-setting is disproportionately prevalent in individuals with intellectual disability, requiring adapted assessment and management approaches within specialist services.
Social isolation, peer victimisation, and poor social integration during development reduce protective factors and increase the probability that fire-setting becomes an affectively salient outlet.
A history of multiple fire-setting episodes is the strongest individual predictor of future fire-setting; recidivism rates in fire-setters without treatment are substantial.

affectedDemographics: Males in childhood, adolescence, and early adulthood — the dominant clinical presentation
Individuals with ADHD or other neurodevelopmental conditions
People with intellectual disability or learning difficulties
Adults in forensic settings with arson convictions and co-occurring substance use disorder
Individuals with adverse childhood experience histories and emotional dysregulation
Those with broader impulse-control disorder profiles involving multiple domains
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosing pyromania requires careful differentiation from the much more common categories of fire-setting for instrumental reasons, fire-setting in the context of conduct disorder or antisocial personality, fire-setting associated with psychosis, and fire-setting related to intellectual disability. The core diagnostic criteria are: multiple acts of deliberate fire-setting; the presence of an affective tension–relief–pleasure cycle associated with fire; specific fascination with fire beyond the instrumental act; and the exclusion of other motivations and psychiatric causes. A structured clinical interview explores the detailed history of each fire-setting episode, the emotional antecedents and consequences, the subjective experience during the act, and any motivational content. Validated instruments include the Arson Risk Assessment (ROSH Fire) and structured fire-setting specific tools used in forensic services. Assessment of co-occurring psychiatric conditions — ADHD, substance use disorder, conduct disorder, intellectual disability, mood disorders — is essential. Forensic risk assessment addresses the likelihood of future fire-setting using actuarial tools alongside clinical judgment. A thorough developmental history, including childhood fire exposure, family history of fire-setting, and trauma history, is standard. In adults presenting following an arson charge, the assessment is conducted in collaboration with legal and probationary services. Brain imaging is not part of routine diagnosis but may be indicated if organic causes of disinhibition are suspected.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Establishes whether fire-setting meets the full diagnostic criteria for pyromania by systematically evaluating motivations, affective pattern, fascination with fire, and exclusion of alternative explanations.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A psychiatrist or forensic psychologist conducts a detailed interview of 60–90 minutes covering the history of fire-setting, emotional antecedents and consequences, and full psychiatric history.

diagnosticTests.test: Fire-specific risk assessment (ROSH Fire or equivalent)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Structured evaluation of fire-setting risk, motivation typology, and protective factors to guide management intensity and supervision level.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A trained forensic clinician reviews documented history alongside interview findings; typically used in forensic and correctional settings following a fire-setting offence.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies co-occurring ADHD, substance use disorder, intellectual disability, antisocial personality, mood disorder, and psychosis, all of which significantly alter diagnosis and management.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A systematic interview using standardised diagnostic criteria taking 60–90 minutes; may be supplemented with cognitive assessment and self-report questionnaires.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates intellectual functioning, executive function, impulsivity, and working memory, identifying neurodevelopmental contributors and guiding adapted treatment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standardised cognitive tests administered over 1–2 hours by a psychologist; includes intelligence assessment and specific executive function measures.

diagnosticTests.test: Impulse-control and personality assessment (PCL-R, Barratt Impulsiveness Scale)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies impulsivity and antisocial personality features, distinguishing pyromania from conduct disorder and antisocial personality disorder as the primary driver of fire-setting.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A combination of structured interview and validated self-report scales; results interpreted in the context of the full clinical picture.

treatmentOverview: Treatment for pyromania is multimodal and must be tailored to the individual's age, cognitive ability, co-occurring conditions, and forensic status. Cognitive-behavioural therapy is the primary psychological approach, addressing the specific maintaining factors of pyromania: identifying and interrupting the tension–urge–relief cycle through urge surfing and stimulus control techniques; addressing cognitive distortions about the pleasure or harmlessness of fire-setting; fire safety education and perspective-taking regarding the impact on victims; and developing alternative coping strategies for the emotional states that precede fire-setting urges. For children and adolescents, fire-setting intervention programmes — developed by fire services in partnership with mental health — provide psychoeducation and skills training in structured formats. Pharmacological treatment targets co-occurring conditions: stimulant medication or non-stimulant alternatives for ADHD, SSRIs or mood stabilisers for co-occurring mood or impulse-control dysregulation, and naltrexone where preliminary evidence suggests reduction in urge intensity in impulse-control disorders. No pharmacological agent has been specifically licensed for pyromania, and prescribing is guided by identified comorbidities. Family therapy and parent training are critical components for child and adolescent presentations. Residential or inpatient settings may be necessary where community risk is unacceptable. Long-term relapse prevention planning with supervision by forensic community mental health teams is standard in post-conviction management. Treatment engagement is complicated in forensic populations by involuntary context and institutional barriers, making motivational interviewing an important precursor to structured therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Cognitive-behavioural therapy (CBT)
medicalTreatments.description: The primary evidence-based psychological approach: identifies and breaks the tension–urge–fire-setting cycle, addresses fire fascination cognitions, builds alternative coping strategies for affective dysregulation, incorporates fire safety psychoeducation, and develops a personalised relapse prevention plan. Adapted versions exist for children, adolescents, and those with intellectual disability.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Studies of fire-setting specific CBT programmes in juvenile and adult populations report recidivism reductions of 40–50% at one-year follow-up; robust RCT evidence is limited by the rarity of the condition.

medicalTreatments.name: Stimulant medication or atomoxetine for co-occurring ADHD
medicalTreatments.description: Methylphenidate, amphetamine salts, or atomoxetine address the impulsivity and inhibitory deficits that amplify pyromanic urges in the majority of patients who meet criteria for ADHD; stimulant prescribing requires careful risk assessment given the potential for misuse.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Treatment of ADHD in fire-setters with comorbid ADHD shows significant reduction in impulsive behaviour across domains; direct evidence for fire-setting reduction is from clinical series rather than RCTs.

medicalTreatments.name: SSRIs and mood stabilisers
medicalTreatments.description: Used to manage co-occurring depression, OCD features, or affective dysregulation that drives tension states preceding fire-setting urges; valproate, lithium, or lamotrigine may be used when mood instability is a maintaining factor.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: SSRIs show 40–60% response for co-occurring depression and OCD; mood stabilisers show benefit in broader impulse-control disorder populations though specific pyromania RCTs are lacking.

medicalTreatments.name: Naltrexone
medicalTreatments.description: An opioid receptor antagonist that reduces the reward-driven relief associated with impulsive behaviours; preliminary case series and open-label data in impulse-control disorders suggest reduction in urge intensity and frequency, providing a rationale for off-label use in pyromania.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Small case series report 50–70% reduction in urge intensity for kleptomania and other impulse-control disorders; no pyromania-specific controlled trial, but mechanism is plausible.

medicalTreatments.name: Motivational interviewing (MI)
medicalTreatments.description: Used as a precursor to structured CBT, particularly in involuntary forensic contexts, to develop internal motivation for change and reduce ambivalence about giving up fire-setting as a coping strategy; important given low treatment engagement rates in convicted fire-setters.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: MI increases treatment engagement and completion rates in forensic populations by 30–40% compared to treatment as usual in meta-analyses of substance use and offender populations.

alternativeTreatments.name: Fire service partnership programmes (juvenile fire-setter intervention)
alternativeTreatments.description: Collaborative programmes between mental health services and fire services deliver structured psychoeducation about the consequences of fire-setting, fire safety skills, and empathy development with a fire-fighter mentor; evidence from US and UK programme evaluations shows significant recidivism reduction in juvenile fire-setters.

alternativeTreatments.name: Family and parent management training
alternativeTreatments.description: For child and adolescent presentations, parent training in contingency management, emotional coaching, and consistent limit-setting addresses the family system maintaining factors that sustain fire-setting behaviour; combined with individual therapy for the child.

whySeeSpecialist: A forensic psychiatrist, child and adolescent psychiatrist, or clinical psychologist with expertise in impulse-control disorders and fire-setting should be involved whenever pyromania is suspected or following any fire-setting act. Accurate diagnosis distinguishes true pyromania from the much more common instrumental fire-setting and from fire-setting as a symptom of conduct disorder, psychosis, or intellectual disability — a distinction with major management implications. Specialist assessment provides a structured risk evaluation that guides supervision, treatment intensity, and liaison with legal services.
doctorSelectionGuide: For adults, seek a forensic psychiatrist or clinical psychologist with experience in impulse-control disorders and fire-setting risk assessment, ideally embedded in a forensic community mental health team. For children and adolescents, a child psychiatrist or clinical psychologist experienced in disruptive behaviour disorders and fire-setting intervention programmes is appropriate. Many services work in collaboration with fire services — ask whether the clinician has links to a fire-setter intervention programme. Clarify the interface between clinical treatment and any legal supervision requirements from the outset.
preventionStrategies: Early identification and intervention for fire-setting in children, with referral to specialist fire-setter intervention programmes before behaviour becomes entrenched.
Treatment of ADHD and other impulse-control conditions in childhood that provide the substrate for pyromanic impulse escalation.
Family-based fire safety programmes targeting households where children have been caught setting fires, addressing both behavioural risk and protective parenting strategies.
Alcohol and substance use prevention and treatment, given the major disinhibitory role of substances in fire-setting acts.
Community fire service outreach to schools in high-risk areas, combining fire safety education with identification of children showing excessive fire fascination.
lifestyleModifications: Identify and avoid personal high-risk situations and environmental cues — specific locations, emotional states, or social contexts — that precipitate the tension build-up preceding fire urges.

Develop and rehearse specific alternative coping strategies for the emotional states that precede fire-setting urges: intense exercise, a structured distraction plan, or immediate contact with a support person.
Maintain sobriety: alcohol is a major disinhibitor of fire-setting impulses and removing it from the environment is a critical protective step.
Establish a crisis contact plan with the treatment team to be used when tension is building, before reaching the point of urge to act.
Remove or restrict access to fire-starting materials in the home environment as a stimulus-control strategy.
Engage in structured activity and regular routines that reduce unstructured time and boredom associated with urge escalation.

recommended: Regular balanced meals to maintain stable blood glucose and reduce irritability and impulsivity associated with hypoglycaemia
Adequate hydration; dehydration increases general arousal and reduces cognitive control
avoid: Alcohol — the most important dietary modification given its major disinhibitory effect on fire-setting impulse control; abstinence is strongly recommended
High-dose stimulant substances including excessive caffeine and illicit stimulants that increase arousal and reduce inhibitory control

exerciseGuidelines: Regular aerobic exercise — at least 150 minutes of moderate-intensity activity per week — is recommended as an adjunct to treatment for pyromania. Exercise reduces physiological arousal, improves mood regulation, and provides a constructive outlet for the tension states that precede fire-setting urges. High-intensity exercise immediately following the onset of pyromanic urges may serve as a competing response that reduces urge intensity. No specific exercise restrictions are associated with the disorder; improving physical fitness also supports engagement with structured daily routines.
dailyManagement: Use a structured urge-monitoring diary recording tension level, emotional state, time, and location; patterns identified in therapy become specific targets for intervention.
Apply urge-surfing techniques from CBT when tension begins to build: observe the urge without acting, noting its rise and natural fall when not reinforced.
Maintain the crisis safety plan at all times and use it without hesitation when tension reaches a predefined threshold.
Adhere precisely to medication regimens for co-occurring ADHD or mood disorders, as impulsivity control depends on consistent pharmacological management.
Communicate proactively with your clinical and supervision team, especially after high-risk periods or urge escalation, rather than waiting for scheduled appointments.
prognosis: Prognosis for pyromania depends heavily on early identification, treatment engagement, co-occurring conditions, and the forensic context. Juvenile fire-setters who receive structured intervention programmes have substantially lower recidivism rates than those who do not; evidence from US fire-setter intervention programme evaluations reports recidivism reductions of 40–60% in treated juvenile populations. Adult pyromania with co-occurring ADHD and substance use disorder responds to multimodal management but carries a higher chronic recidivism risk than juvenile presentations. Prognostic factors favouring improvement include younger age, higher intelligence, absence of antisocial personality disorder, treatment engagement, and effective social support. Pure pyromania without antisocial features and with high motivation for change has the most favourable prognosis of the fire-setting subtypes. Long-term management is typically required; full remission without ongoing support is uncommon in forensic populations.
recoveryTimeline: Fire-setting frequency and urge intensity typically begin to reduce within the first 8–12 weeks of structured CBT combined with treatment of co-occurring ADHD or mood disorder. Consolidation of relapse prevention skills, development of alternative coping strategies, and addressing the full maintaining-factor profile takes 6–12 months of active treatment. Long-term maintenance, including periodic booster sessions and ongoing supervision in forensic cases, is standard practice for adults with multiple fire-setting episodes.
complications: Criminal prosecution and incarceration for arson — fire-setting carries severe legal consequences, including lengthy custodial sentences, that have permanent social and employment effects.
Catastrophic harm to others: fire-setting can cause deaths, serious injuries, destruction of homes or businesses, and community-wide trauma.
Severe depression and suicidal ideation secondary to shame, legal consequences, and chronic impairment in social and occupational functioning.
Repeated fire-setting with escalating scale or frequency in untreated cases, increasing risk of catastrophic outcomes.

Iatrogenic harm from inadequate assessment: incorrect diagnosis of pyromania when fire-setting is actually driven by psychosis, severe intellectual disability, or conduct disorder — requiring fundamentally different management.
Social exclusion, family breakdown, and lifelong stigmatisation following fire-related offences, compounding psychiatric morbidity.

supportResources.name: NHS — Pyromania and Fire-Setting
supportResources.description: NHS patient information on pyromania, its symptoms, diagnosis, and treatment options available through NHS mental health services.

supportResources.name: International Association of Arson Investigators (IAAI)
supportResources.description: Professional organisation for fire investigation providing educational resources on fire-setting behaviour and fire investigation standards.

supportResources.name: NICE — Antisocial Behaviour and Conduct Disorders in Children and Young People (CG158)
supportResources.description: NICE guideline covering management of conduct and impulse-control disorders in young people, relevant to juvenile fire-setter assessment and intervention.

sources.title: Lejoyeux M et al. Pyromania and Pathological Fire-Setting in Adults: A Review. Comprehensive Psychiatry, 2002
Gauthier JM and Lignugaris-Kraft B. Efficacy of a Juvenile Firesetters Intervention Program. Canadian Journal of Criminology, 2002

confusedWithConditions.name: Conduct disorder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: In conduct disorder, fire-setting is one of multiple antisocial acts driven by disregard for rules and others' rights; in pyromania the specific tension–fascination–relief cycle with fire defines the motivation. Conduct disorder is more common and does not require the affective fire-fascination pattern.

confusedWithConditions.name: Intermittent explosive disorder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Intermittent explosive disorder involves recurrent uncontrolled aggressive outbursts out of proportion to provocation; pyromania involves deliberate, premeditated fire-setting with fire-specific fascination. Both are impulse-control disorders but involve different behaviours and arousal patterns.

confusedWithConditions.name: Kleptomania
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Kleptomania involves an uncontrollable urge to steal objects without financial need; pyromania involves an uncontrollable urge to set fires. Both share the tension–relief impulse cycle and are classified together in ICD-10 impulse disorder categories.

relatedConditions.name: Kleptomania
relatedConditions.relevance: Kleptomania shares the same ICD-10 impulse-control disorder classification and the same tension–pleasure–relief cycle; both conditions respond to overlapping CBT and pharmacological approaches.

relatedConditions.name: Intermittent explosive disorder
relatedConditions.relevance: Another impulse-control disorder characterised by failure to resist urges resulting in harmful acts; shares neurobiological substrates of impulsivity and serotonergic dysregulation.

relatedConditions.name: Attention deficit hyperactivity disorder
relatedConditions.relevance: ADHD is the most common comorbidity in pyromania and fire-setting populations; impulsivity amplifies the core pyromanic impulse and must be treated concurrently.

relatedConditions.name: Impulse control disorder
relatedConditions.relevance: Pyromania falls within the broader impulse-control disorder category; understanding the shared impulse-regulation deficit informs treatment strategy.

faqs.question: What is pyromania?
faqs.answer: Pyromania (ICD-10: F63.1) is an impulse-control disorder characterised by recurrent, deliberate fire-setting preceded by mounting tension and followed by pleasure or relief at the time of setting or witnessing the fire. It is not motivated by financial gain, revenge, or any external purpose — the fire-setting is itself the objective.

faqs.question: Is pyromania the same as arson?
faqs.answer: No. Arson is a legal term for deliberately setting fire to property for any reason; most arsonists do not have pyromania. True pyromania — where fire-setting is driven purely by the tension–fascination–relief cycle — is found in fewer than 3–4% of arson arrestees. Most fire-setting is motivated by financial gain, revenge, or other instrumental reasons.

faqs.question: What causes pyromania?
faqs.answer: Pyromania arises from a combination of biological impulsivity, conditioned affective associations with fire from early experiences, adverse childhood environments, and frequently co-occurring ADHD or other neurodevelopmental conditions. Substance use — especially alcohol — significantly disinhibits the fire-setting impulse in those already predisposed.

faqs.question: How common is pyromania?
faqs.answer: True pyromania meeting strict ICD-10 criteria is rare: fewer than 3–4% of arson arrestees meet full diagnostic criteria when carefully assessed. Fire-setting behaviour broadly is more common, affecting approximately 1% of community samples, but most of this is driven by motivations other than the specific tension–fascination–relief cycle of pyromania.

faqs.question: Can pyromania be treated?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cognitive-behavioural therapy targeting the urge cycle and coping strategies is the primary treatment, combined with pharmacotherapy for co-occurring ADHD, mood disorders, or substance use. Juvenile fire-setter intervention programmes show 40–60% recidivism reduction. Treatment is most effective when begun early before multiple episodes occur.

faqs.question: What is the difference between pyromania and conduct disorder?
faqs.answer: In conduct disorder, fire-setting is one of multiple antisocial behaviours driven by disregard for rules and others. In pyromania, fire-setting is specifically driven by a fascination with fire and a tension–relief arousal pattern. Distinguishing the two is clinically critical because management differs substantially.

faqs.question: How is pyromania diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires a structured clinical interview establishing: multiple deliberate fire-setting episodes; a specific affective tension–pleasure–relief cycle associated with fire; fascination with fire; and exclusion of other motivations and psychiatric disorders. Forensic risk assessment tools and comorbidity evaluation are part of specialist assessment.

faqs.question: What medications help with pyromania?
faqs.answer: No medication is specifically licensed for pyromania. Stimulants or atomoxetine treat co-occurring ADHD; SSRIs or mood stabilisers address mood dysregulation; naltrexone may reduce urge intensity based on preliminary impulse-control disorder evidence. All pharmacotherapy targets co-occurring conditions or the general impulse-control deficit.

faqs.question: Is pyromania more common in children or adults?
faqs.answer: Pyromania and fire-setting broadly are more prevalent in children and adolescents and tend to decline with age, but adult pyromania occurs and is more commonly seen in forensic settings. Childhood onset is the strongest developmental risk factor for persistent fire-setting into adulthood.

faqs.question: Does pyromania involve fascination with fire?
faqs.answer: Yes. Specific fascination with fire — its appearance, sensory qualities, and contexts such as fire stations and emergency vehicles — is a diagnostic feature of pyromania. This distinguishes it from fire-setting for financial or other instrumental reasons, where fire is a means rather than an object of specific interest.

faqs.question: Is ADHD linked to pyromania?
faqs.answer: Yes. ADHD is the most consistently identified psychiatric comorbidity in pyromania and fire-setting populations. Impulsivity and deficits in inhibitory control associated with ADHD amplify the difficulty of resisting pyromanic urges once tension builds. Treating ADHD is a core component of pyromania management in those with comorbid diagnosis.

faqs.question: What happens during a psychiatric assessment for pyromania?
faqs.answer: A forensic psychiatrist or psychologist takes a detailed history of each fire-setting episode, the emotional states before and after, motivations, fascination with fire, and full psychiatric history. Co-occurring conditions are assessed systematically. In forensic contexts, risk assessment tools are used and findings are shared with legal and supervision services.

faqs.question: Can children with fire-setting behaviour recover?
faqs.answer: Yes. Children who receive structured intervention — fire-setter intervention programmes, family therapy, and treatment of co-occurring ADHD — have substantially lower rates of persistent fire-setting than untreated children. Early intervention is the most important prognostic factor; recidivism rates in treated juvenile programmes are 40–60% lower than untreated comparisons.

faqs.question: What should I do if a child is setting fires?
faqs.answer: A single serious or repeated fire-setting incident in a child warrants referral for psychiatric and psychological assessment. Contact your GP, paediatrician, or child mental health service. Fire services in many areas run juvenile fire-setter intervention programmes in partnership with mental health teams. Do not dismiss fire-setting as experimentation without professional assessment.

faqs.question: Is pyromania associated with violence?
faqs.answer: Pyromania is not inherently associated with direct interpersonal violence, but fire-setting itself carries catastrophic risk of harm to others. Pyromania in the context of antisocial personality disorder or substance use is associated with greater risk of serious harm. Each case requires individualised forensic risk assessment.

faqs.question: How does alcohol affect pyromania?
faqs.answer: Alcohol significantly lowers inhibitory control and is one of the most consistent factors associated with fire-setting episodes in susceptible individuals. Reducing or eliminating alcohol intake is a core element of relapse prevention for pyromania and should be addressed as a primary modifiable risk factor in any treatment plan.

faqs.question: What is the prognosis for pyromania?
faqs.answer: Prognosis is most favourable for juvenile fire-setters who receive early intervention — recidivism rates are reduced 40–60% in structured programmes. Adults with pure pyromania, high motivation, and no antisocial personality features have better outcomes. Chronic, forensic cases with multiple co-occurring conditions have higher recidivism risk and require long-term intensive management.

faqs.question: Is pyromania related to kleptomania?
faqs.answer: Both pyromania and kleptomania are classified as impulse-control disorders under ICD-10 and share the same tension–urge–relief cycle as their core mechanism. Both respond to overlapping CBT and pharmacological approaches targeting impulsivity. They can co-occur in the same individual, though each has a distinct behavioural focus.

faqs.question: What relapse prevention strategies are used in pyromania?
faqs.answer: Relapse prevention focuses on identifying personal high-risk situations and emotional states that precede urges, developing alternative coping strategies for those states, removing fire-starting materials, maintaining sobriety, and establishing a crisis plan with immediate contacts. Structured supervision and regular check-ins with the clinical team are part of long-term management.

faqs.question: Does pyromania require inpatient treatment?
faqs.answer: Most pyromania can be managed in community forensic or outpatient settings. Inpatient admission is indicated when community risk is unacceptable — for example, when fire-setting is imminent, the individual is acutely psychotic, severely intoxicated, or cannot be safely managed in the community. Inpatient settings provide intensive assessment and short-term risk containment rather than definitive treatment.

metaDescription: Pyromania (F63.1) is a rare impulse disorder causing recurrent fire-setting driven by tension and relief. Learn diagnosis, CBT treatment and risk management.
